title: Irretrievable breakdown of marriage - why it's still not a statutory ground
p1: A marriage can be over in every meaningful way, yet that alone may not be enough to end it legally. That surprises many people because living apart for years does not automatically translate into a divorce.
p2: A common misconception is that courts can grant divorce simply because the relationship has completely failed. In reality, "irretrievable breakdown of marriage" is still not a statutory ground under India's general divorce law. Instead, most couples must rely on existing legal grounds such as cruelty, desertion, adultery, or choose mutual consent if both spouses agree. In a few exceptional cases, the Supreme Court has dissolved marriages by using its special constitutional powers, but that is not the standard route available to every couple.
p3: This distinction matters because a judgment delivered under extraordinary constitutional powers does not automatically change what the statute says. Many people read headlines about such cases and assume the law has changed when it has not. The practical takeaway is simple: never rely on a news headline to understand your legal options. The legal ground available in your case can be very different from what appears in a landmark judgment.
conclusion: Do you think irretrievable breakdown of marriage should become a statutory ground for divorce, or should the current legal framework remain unchanged?
